summary: This very brief post shares a humorous quote from a Wired article about someone whose life was profoundly shaped by the Street Fighter character Blanka. The quoted person admits to trying Blanka's fighting moves in real fights (unsuccessfully) and credits the character with helping him meet his wife. Atwood adds a dry observation about the impracticality of Blanka's signature electric attack.
key_points: Video game characters can have unexpectedly profound impacts on players' real lives and identities
The gap between game fantasy and real-world application is often comically wide
Gaming culture shapes personal identity in ways that extend far beyond the screen
money_quotes: When I got into real fights, I even tried using some of his moves. They never worked.
I often ask myself, what would Blanka do? I even met my wife because of this game. So yes, I owe my whole life to Blanka.
I'm thinking that electricity move might be particularly difficult to pull off.
key_insight: The devotion gamers feel toward fictional characters can shape their real-world identities and life paths in surprisingly meaningful ways.
